comments.text: Let $F_r(n) := \max\{|E(G)| : |V(G)| = n,\ 1 \le b(e) \le r-1 \text{ for every } e \in E(G)\}$, where $b(e)$ is the number of triangles containing the edge $e$. The threshold in the problem is $e(n,r) = F_r(n) + 1$, so the two questions become whether $F_{r+1}(n) - F_r(n) \to \infty$ and whether $F_{r+1}(n)/F_r(n) \to 1$. For the first nontrivial step, $r = 2$ to $r = 3$, the following reduction may be useful. Any graph admissible for $F_2(n)$ has every edge in exactly one triangle. For two vertices $u,v$ write $c(u,v) := |N(u) \cap N(v)|$. If $G$ attains $F_2(n)$ and contains a non-edge $uv$ with $1 \le c(u,v) \le 2$, then adding $uv$ yields a graph admissible for $F_3(n)$: the new edge lies in $c(u,v) \le 2$ triangles, each old edge used in those triangles rises from booksize $1$ to booksize $2$, and no other booksize changes. The result has $F_2(n)+1$ edges, so the existence of even one such non-edge in some extremal graph for $F_2(n)$ gives $F_3(n) > F_2(n)$. A necessary obstruction is therefore that every extremal graph for $F_2(n)$ has $c(u,v) = 0$ or $c(u,v) \ge 3$ for every non-edge $uv$; that is, codegree $1$ occurs only on edges and codegree $2$ nowhere. So $F_3(n) > F_2(n)$ is tied to whether extremal uniquely-triangulated graphs can avoid all non-edges of codegree $1$ or $2$. A separate observation suggests the difficulty is not uniform in $r$. Let $H$ be locally linear, every edge in exactly one triangle, and let $H^{(t)}$ be its blow-up, each vertex replaced by an independent set of size $t$ and each edge by a complete bipartite graph. Then every edge of $H^{(t)}$ lies in exactly $t$ triangles, and each triangle of $H$ becomes an edge-disjoint copy of $K_{t,t,t}$, so booksizes are decided inside single copies. For $t \ge 3$ the edges of $K_{t,t,t}$ can be reduced from booksize $t$ to booksize at most $t-1$, with every edge still in a triangle, by deleting two diagonal matchings, $2t$ of the $3t^2$ edges, a fraction $\tfrac{2}{3t}$. For $t = 2$ the copy is the octahedron $K_{2,2,2}$, which is not locally linear, and any locally linear subgraph omits a positive fraction of its edges; two disjoint triangles realise $6$ of the $12$. So within the blow-up family the cost of stepping one booksize level down is of order $1/t$, smallest for large $t$ and a positive constant at the $r = 2$ to $r = 3$ step. These constructions bound only a restricted family from below and do not control $F_r(n)$ itself, so they do not settle either question; they do suggest the $r = 2$ to $r = 3$ case is the hardest.
